# LLM-Assisted Engineering Estimation
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

A citation-backed methodology for estimating software development work when LLMs write the implementation code and humans orchestrate via specs. Designed for teams using spec-driven AI workflows (spec-kit, CLAUDE.md, Claude Code) where AI generates the vast majority of implementation code. Synthesized from 84 sources including 8 RCTs, 17 industry reports, and research from Google, Microsoft, Anthropic, and independent organizations.

## The Problem

Traditional estimation assumes humans write code. In spec-driven AI development, humans write specifications and AI writes implementation — the human role shifts from implementer to orchestrator. This changes what takes time: the bottleneck moves from coding to context preparation (specs, CLAUDE.md, task decomposition), verification (review, testing, comprehension), and non-code work (meetings, deployment, stakeholder communication) that AI does not accelerate.

**Why this matters for estimation:**
- Without structured specs, AI makes experienced developers 19% *slower* (METR RCT [3])
- With proper specs, AI achieves 95% first-attempt accuracy (Red Hat [57]) and 21-56% coding speedup ([1][4])
- But coding is only 25-35% of delivery [38] — even large coding gains have modest total impact
- Industry-wide, individual AI gains (+21% tasks) do not translate to organizational throughput [27]
- Developers overestimate AI's benefit by 39-44 percentage points [3]

## Target Audience

This methodology assumes teams where:
- AI writes **most or all implementation code** (via Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot with agentic workflows)
- Humans write **specifications** before AI generates code (spec-kit, detailed prompts, CLAUDE.md)
- The workflow follows **Specify -> Plan -> Implement -> Verify**, not ad-hoc prompting
- Context engineering (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, project conventions) is invested in as infrastructure

Industry average AI code share is 42% [28]. This methodology targets teams at 80-100%.

## Quick Decision Framework

### 1. Classify by Spec + Task Type

| Category | Spec Quality | Task Type | Estimate Adjustment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Detailed spec exists | Greenfield, CRUD, boilerplate, test scaffolding | 0.50x - 0.70x of traditional |
| **B** | Basic spec / CLAUDE.md | Feature work, refactoring, API integration | 0.65x - 0.85x of traditional |
| **C** | No spec / ad-hoc prompting | Same tasks as A/B but without specs | 0.90x - 1.15x (spec absence negates AI benefit) |
| **D** | Any | Architecture, complex debugging, cross-system integration | 1.0x - 1.25x (human judgment dominates) |

**Key difference from industry data:** Categories A and B assume spec-driven workflow. The METR -19% finding [3] reflects Category C (no specs). Teams with good specs should see Category A/B results, not Category C.

### 2. Estimate the Spec-Driven SDLC

In a spec-driven AI workflow, the SDLC phases shift:

| Phase | Traditional Share | Spec-Driven AI Share | Who |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specification & context prep | 5-10% | **20-30%** | Human |
| Implementation (coding) | 25-35% | **5-15%** (AI generates, human iterates) | AI + Human |
| Verification (review, testing, security) | 20-30% | **25-35%** (more code to verify) | Human + AI-assisted |
| Non-code (meetings, deployment, docs, stakeholders) | 25-40% | **25-35%** (largely unchanged) | Human |

### 3. Apply the Estimate

```
Total = Spec_Time + (AI_Implementation x Task_Adjustment) + Verification_Time + Non_Code_Time
```

- **Spec time:** 15-60 min per task depending on complexity; front-loaded investment
- **Task adjustment:** 0.50x - 1.25x from classification table above
- **Verification:** Budget 1.0x - 2.0x of AI implementation time (review against spec, testing)
- **Non-code:** Largely unchanged from traditional estimates

### 4. Budget Non-Code Work

| Activity | AI Impact in Spec-Driven Workflow |
|---|---|
| Test writing | AI generates tests from specs; human reviews. Budget 0.5x - 0.8x of traditional |
| Test execution / CI | Unchanged (infrastructure constraint) |
| Code review | Review against spec is more structured than open-ended review. Budget 1.0x - 1.5x |
| Documentation | AI drafts from specs; human revises. Budget 0.3x - 0.5x of traditional |
| Sprint ceremonies | Unchanged (~25% of sprint) |
| Deployment / release | Unchanged |
| Security review | Budget 1.3x - 1.5x (AI code has 2.74x more vulnerabilities [32]) |

### 5. Use Measured Velocity, Not Self-Reports

Developers believe AI helps 20-24% when it may actually slow them down 19% without specs [3]. Track actual sprint outcomes for 2-3 months before trusting new velocity numbers.

## Key Numbers

| Finding | Value | Context | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-attempt accuracy with specs | 95% | Spec-driven workflow | Red Hat [57] |
| First-attempt accuracy without specs | 70-82% | Ad-hoc prompting | Benchmarks |
| Experienced devs slower without specs | +19% completion time | Unstructured AI use | METR RCT [3] |
| Coding speedup (greenfield, with specs) | +21% to +56% | Controlled experiments | [1][4] |
| Perception gap | 39-44 pp | Universal | METR RCT [3] |
| Coding share of traditional SDLC | 25-35% | Pre-AI baseline | Bain 2025 [38] |
| System-level ceiling (tool addition) | ~10% | Adding AI tools to existing workflow | Six studies [27] |
| System-level ceiling (workflow transformation) | 25-30% | End-to-end process change with AI | Bain 2025 [38] |
| AI code defect rate | 1.7x more issues | All AI-generated code | CodeRabbit [31] |
| Context miss rate | 65% of developers | Without structured context | Qodo [30] |

